content.full_text:
emorandon

To : Ted, Bob S. (Jay) Jeanne, Helen, Alan                    Date : November 6, 1

M-81-157

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Lynn

Subject: Independent Expenditures (Rose Bird Recall, Faldman, et. al.)

     If an officeholder makes independent expenditures from personal
funds, it looks like the officeholder would not have to file an inde-
pendent expenditure report because §4208 only refers to committees and
we have never defined an officeholder as a committee.

     This came up when I was reviewing Jeanne's advice to Palmer
Madden (see attached) regarding the Bird recall. If Rose Bird had
an on-going controlled committee that made expenditures to oppose
the recall, the committee would be required to file an independent
expenditure report on the 35 day qualification schedule. But if she
made independent expenditures from personal funds to oppose the recall,
I don't think an independent expenditure report is required.

     This also brings up the requirements again for Marvin Feldman's
committee and the primarily formed recall committee. If Feldman's
committee is not primarily formed to support the recall as he claims,
then all expenditures made by the committee are independent expendi-
tures in support of the recall (since the expenditures are not being
made at the behest of the primarily formed committee that Feldman is
at war with) and Feldman's committee would also file an independent
expenditure report on the qualification schedule.

     This seems to be how the whole thing looks now. The primarily
formed committee would file a 35 day qualification statement sometime
in 1982 and would then file on the threshold schedule whether or not
the recall qualified because any election for the recall would most
likely be held in June or November. Feldman's committee would file
statements on a threshold schedule for 1981 & 1982, but would also file
an independent expenditure report on the 35 day qualification schedule.

     Any on-going committee controlled by Rose Bird would follow the
same schedule as outlined for Feldman's committee if it makes expen-
ditures to oppose the recall. Any new committee primarily formed to
oppose the recall and controlled by Rose Bird would file as a primarily
formed committee, 35 day qualification statement and then threshold
schedule. Rose Bird, however, would only file threshold statements
for 1981 and 1982 even if she made expenditures from personal funds.

     What do you think? Confusing enough for you?

FROM: Jeanne

DATE: 10/23/81

JLYNN

[ ] BARBARA

[ ] IRICH

[ ] BOB S.

[ ]HELEN

[ ] OTHER TED

[XX]TELEPHONE ADVICE - SEE BELOW

[ ]CORRESPONDENCE ADVICE - SEE ATTACHED DRAFT
(IF YOU HAVE ANY COMMENTS, NOTIFY ADVISOR WITHIN 1 DAY)

FILE LOCATION:

INCLUDE IN ADVICE PACKAGE:        YES [ ]        NO [x]


CALLER Palmer Madden

REPRESENTING

PHONE NUMBER: 415 937-8000


QUESTION: (1) Why is the effort to qualify a recall election subject to
campaign disclosure provisions? (2) If a group of peole start
collecting and spending money to oppose the qualification are the
a committee? (3) When do they file? (4) If the Chief Justice
spends money out of her existing committee, what are her filing
requirements? Personal funds? Forms a new committee to oppose
the qualification?

ANSWER:
(1) Definition of "measure" - 82043 (2) Yes - 84200(c)

(3) First report is 35-day statement; after that filing schedule
determined by election date if qualifies; (4) No change in filing
dates until and if she becomes a candidate by virtue of qualifica-
tion of recall election, then filing schedule determined by date of
election; same for personal funds used; if she forms new
committee, will be subject to 84200(d) - must file 35-day statement
then filing schedule determined by date of election.

sections.question:
(1) Why is the effort to qualify a recall election subject to campaign disclosure provisions? (2) If a group of people start collecting and spending money to oppose the qualification are they a committee? (3) When do they file? (4) If the Chief Justice spends money out of her existing committee, what are her filing requirements? Personal funds? Forms a new committee to oppose the qualification?

ANSWER:
(1) Definition of "measure" - 82043   (2) Yes - 84200(c)
(3) First report is 35-day statement; after that filing schedule determined by election date if qualifies; (4) No change in filing dates until if she becomes a candidate by virtue of qualification of recall election, then filing schedule determined by date of election; same for personal funds used; if she forms new committee, will be subject to 84200(d) - must file 35-day statement then filing schedule determined by date of election.
sections.conclusion: (1) Definition of "measure" - 82043   (2) Yes - 84200(c) (3) First report is 35-day statement; after that filing schedule determined by election date if qualifies; (4) No change in filing dates until if she becomes a candidate by virtue of qualification of recall election, then filing schedule determined by date of election; same for personal funds used; if she forms new committee, will be subject to 84200(d) - must file 35-day statement then filing schedule determined by date of election.
sections.question_synthetic: The caller asked four related questions about campaign disclosure requirements for a recall election effort: why recall qualification efforts are subject to disclosure, whether opponents of the recall qualify as a committee, when filing requirements commence, and what filing obligations the Chief Justice has under different spending scenarios.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Recall qualification efforts are subject to disclosure under the definition of "measure" in section 82043. Groups spending money to oppose qualification constitute a committee under section 84200(c) and must file a 35-day statement, followed by filings based on the election date if the recall qualifies. The Chief Justice's filing obligations depend on her spending method: existing committee and personal funds require no change until she becomes a candidate upon qualification; a new committee formed to oppose qualification triggers section 84200(d) requirements including the 35-day statement.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document contains both a detailed memorandum discussion and a separate telephone advice section with structured Q&A format. The telephone advice section provides the formal ANSWER; the memorandum provides additional context and explanation. Caller identified as Palmer Madden representing the Chief Justice's position. Document marked for telephone advice, not included in advice package per checkbox.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: (1) Why is the effort to qualify a recall election subject to campaign disclosure provisions? (2) If a group of people start collecting and spending money to oppose the qualification are they a committee? (3) When do they file? (4) If the Chief Justice spends money out of her existing committee, what are her filing requirements? Personal funds? Forms a new committee to oppose the qualification?

ANSWER:
(1) Definition of "measure" - 82043   (2) Yes - 84200(c)
(3) First report is 35-day statement; after that filing schedule determined by election date if qualifies; (4) No change in filing dates until if she becomes a candidate by virtue of qualification of recall election, then filing schedule determined by date of election; same for personal funds used; if she forms new committee, will be subject to 84200(d) - must file 35-day statement then filing schedule determined by date of election.

CONCLUSION: (1) Definition of "measure" - 82043   (2) Yes - 84200(c) (3) First report is 35-day statement; after that filing schedule determined by election date if qualifies; (4) No change in filing dates until if she becomes a candidate by virtue of qualification of recall election, then filing schedule determined by date of election; same for personal funds used; if she forms new committee, will be subject to 84200(d) - must file 35-day statement then filing schedule determined by date of election.

embedding.summary: This FPPC advice letter addresses campaign disclosure and filing requirements for the Rose Bird recall election effort in 1981, providing guidance on committee status, filing deadlines, and obligations for officials making independent expenditures related to recall qualification.
